steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The goal is to compare Fodor’s hypothesis with Carruthers’s. The key difference from the notes: Fodor says only low-level systems (perception, language) are modular — high-level systems are not. Carruthers’s MMH extends modularity to all cognitive systems. A correct answer must place both hypotheses side by side and highlight this difference in scope.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘In considering some but not all cognitive systems modular, Fodorian modularity is not as expansive in its definition of modularity as MMH is.’: Directly compares both hypotheses on the same dimension (which systems count as modular) and names the clear difference in scope. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Following Fodor’s 1983 hypothesis, Carruthers proposed that modularity of mind includes all cognitive systems.’: Describes Carruthers sequentially after Fodor but doesn’t frame the two as a comparison — it just narrates timeline. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘The hypotheses … differ in whether they consider low-level cognitive systems … modular.’: Factually wrong — both Fodor and Carruthers consider low-level systems modular. The disagreement is about high-level systems. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘… Carruthers … disagreeing with Fodor’s earlier hypothesis that the mind is composed of innate neural structures.’: Misrepresents the disagreement; both accept that modules are innate neural structures — the dispute is about which systems are modular. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
